entry.title: Issue – Purchase Requisition Creation for Lean Services in SAP PS
entry.content:
Hi experts,

We are currently encountering an issue when generating Purchase Requisitions from components in SAP Project System for Lean Services. The scenario concerns materials of type SRV with Product Type Group 2 (Lean Services), within a project and network configured in Enhanced Procurement Mode. This mode has been correctly activated at both the Project Profile level and the Network/Plant level.

The first issue observed is the absence of any option related to services in the Project Builder user-specific settings. When navigating to Project Builder and accessing the user-specific options, only standard processable objects such as materials and activities are available. No setting allows services or lean services to be defined as processable objects.

The second issue occurs during the creation of a Purchase Requisition from a material component using Lean Service ( materials of type SRV with Product Type Group 2 ). When attempting to generate the PR, the system displays an error message requesting the entry of a “Validity Start Date.” However, this field is not available or visible in the Project Builder interface for either the component or the related network activity, making it impossible for the user to provide the required information.

This behavior is observed exclusively for Lean Services, as non stocked material components do not trigger the same error and function correctly.

Experts, could you please help identify the root cause of this issue and propose an appropriate solution?

Hi, 

I am using Flexible Programming Model for my Fiori Application with RAP in Backend. The initial plan was to use the custom pages and building blocks ( as opposed to Fiori Elements Floorplans List Report and Object Page) .  I added a macros:Form  within a Custom Page (using sap.m.Page, but sap.fe.macros.Page does not work either) to display fields for each business object. But the generic actions like Edit,Delete, Save, Discard Draft are not visible on the UI. Once I create an Object page and the form that way, those generic actions are available and working. My question is if it absolutely necessary to use the floorplans for the CRUD Operations be handled by the framework, or is there a way to make it work without implementing every single generic action manually?

<Page
        id="Invoice"
        title="{i18n>InvoiceTitle}"
    >
       
        <macros:Form id='headData' metaPath="@com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.FieldGroup#General"></macros:Form>
 
     
    </Page>

entry.title: Re: Agrupamento de itens MRP na ordem via MD01
entry.content:
Olá @Dayanna_Paris ,
Não, o SAP MRP não permite agrupar vários materiais em uma única ordem planejada ou requisição.

A transação MD01 cria uma ordem planejada para cada necessidade de material.

A transação MD15 pode converter várias ordens planejadas de uma só vez, mas cada uma ainda se torna uma linha de requisição separada.

O agrupamento só ocorre posteriormente: você pode combinar requisições em uma única ordem de compra durante o processo de aquisição (por exemplo, ME21N/ME59N).

Portanto, a solução padrão mais próxima é a conversão coletiva com agrupamento na etapa da ordem de compra.

entry.title: Re: New GL: account assignment table
entry.content:
For your CDS view

If you’re still on ECC with New GL:

  • You’ll need to join FAGLFLEXA (GL line items) with COEP (CO line items) using common fields like document number, fiscal year, and line item.
  • That’s how you retrieve the CO object assignment for P&L accounts.

If you’re on S/4HANA 

  • Use ACDOCA directly. It already includes both FI and CO dimensions, so you don’t need to join separate tables.
  • Fields like KOSTL (cost center), AUFNR (order), PRCTR (profit center), etc. are available in ACDOCA.

entry.title: NF-e Mapping Error: J_1BNFE_CF013 - Invalid Character in Decimal Field
entry.content:
Hey guys,

I am facing a persistent mapping error when processing an NF-e from Brazil in an S/4HANA environment. XML generation stops and the following error message is displayed on the monitor:

"Error mapping the NF-e. The character - is not a decimal digit, a decimal point or the exponential notation "e"."

Situation analysis:

The J_1BNFE_CF013 error occurs when executing output mapping logic, specifically when the system attempts to move a string value containing a hyphen ( -) to a numeric or decimal target field. In our case, the XML is not even generated because the mapping process (probably within a J_1B_NF_MAP_TO_XML function or CL_NFE_PRINT class) fails during the data type conversion.

We check common fields such as Weights (NTGEW/ BRGEW), NCM and State Registration, but the "Character -" error persists even when these fields appear without errors in the ALV display.

Has anyone come across this error?

entry.title: Defaulting “GR Non-Valuated” from PRs to POs
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I would like to seek advice regarding the handling of the “GR Non-Valuated” indicator from PRs to POs.

We would like the “GR Non-Valuated” indicator to be defaulted (ticked) in POs created from PRs with account assignment K (Cost Center).

However, we understand that this behavior cannot be changed via standard configuration, as we cannot change the standard account assignment setup. We have explored the option of creating a custom account assignment category based on K, but Goods Receipt cannot be posted for custom categories, as SAP only supports GR posting for standard account assignment categories (K, P, C, S, F, etc.), as documented in SAP KBA 3387525 (Error M7064)(https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3387525) 

May we kindly confirm whether there are any other supported configuration options to default the “GR Non-Valuated” indicator, or if account assignment categories are the only available approach?

Thank you very much for your guidance.

Thank you.

Best regards, 

HY

entry.title: LGM Knowledge Snippet: Processing Variant Maintenance
entry.content:
In my previous post about Processing Variants, I explained the concept and how they control shipping process behavior in SAP Logistics Management. Now let's dive into the practical side: how do you actually configure and maintain Processing Variants using the Configure Shipping Processes app?

The Configure Shipping Processes App

The Configure Shipping Processes app (find the complete documentation here) is your central tool for managing Processing Variants. This Fiori application provides a clear interface for viewing SAP-delivered variants and creating custom ones tailored to your business requirements.

Viewing Existing Processing Variants

When you open the Configure Shipping Processes app, you'll see a list of all available Processing Variants-both the standard ones delivered by SAP and any custom variants your organization has created.

The list view provides several useful capabilities:

  • Filter by Shipping Process - Quickly narrow down to Parcel, LTL, Direct LTL, FTL, or No Shipping variants
  • Search by Display ID or Description - Find specific variants quickly in large catalogs
  • See SAP-Managed indicator - Immediately identify which variants are delivered by SAP vs. customer-created
  • Check Default status - See which variant is marked as the default fallback
  • View creation and modification details - Showing when variants were created and last changed

SAP delivers five standard Processing Variants out of the box:

  • /SAP/Parcel - Parcel shipments with carrier integrations
  • /SAP/LTL - Consolidated Less-Than-Truckload shipments
  • /SAP/Direct_LTL - Direct LTL with individual carrier confirmation
  • /SAP/No_Shipping - Warehouse-only scenarios

These delivered variants are read-only and serve as tested, stable baselines. You cannot modify them, but you can create your own custom variants based on the same Shipping Processes.

Creating a Custom Processing Variant

When your business has specific requirements that the standard variants don't address, you can create custom Processing Variants. Here's the step-by-step workflow:

Step 1: Start Creation

Click the "Create" button in the Configure Shipping Processes app. The system opens a new draft variant in edit mode.

Step 2: Select the Base Shipping Process

Choose which fundamental Shipping Process your variant will be based on:

  • Parcel - If you need special parcel carrier handling
  • LTL - For consolidated shipments with regular carrier schedules
  • Direct LTL - For shipments requiring explicit carrier confirmation
  • No Shipping - For warehouse-internal processes

This choice is critical because it determines which configuration options will be available in the next steps. The Shipping Process defines the foundational behavior and which attributes you can customize.

Step 3: Define the Display ID

Create a unique Display ID for your variant. Use a clear naming convention that indicates the purpose, for example:

  • "CUSTOM_LTL_EXPRESS" - For expedited LTL shipments
  • "CUSTOM_LTL_CONSOLIDATION" - For LTL with consolidation cutoff enabled
  • "CUSTOM_DIRECTLTL_MANUAL_CONFIRM" - For Direct LTL requiring manual carrier approval

Avoid generic names like "VARIANT_001" or "CUSTOM_1"-your operations team (and maybe also the SAP development team ;-)) will thank you months later when reviewing configurations or troubleshooting.

Step 4: Add a Description

Provide a clear, detailed description explaining:

  • What business scenario this variant supports
  • How it differs from standard SAP variants
  • Any special handling or prerequisites
  • Which organizational units or locations should use it

Note: So far, the delivered processing variants come with translated descriptions, this is not yet available for customer defined processing variants. 

Step 5: Configure Available Options

The app displays configuration options based on the Shipping Process you selected. Not all options are available for all processes-the system only shows settings that are meaningful for your chosen process type.

Currently there is only one option you can maintain (the idea here is of course, to add more step by step):

Consolidation Cutoff (available for LTL and Direct LTL)

Enable this when you want to consolidate multiple Transportation Requests into fewer Consignments during a defined time window. As I explained in the previous Processing Variants post, this dramatically changes process behavior:

  • The system searches for existing open Consignments when creating new Transportation Requests
  • Multiple requests get grouped into the same Consignment
  • Carrier determination happens after the consolidation window closes
  • A Consignment without a carrier is acceptable during consolidation but requires attention afterward

More details about consolidation cutoff can be found in the SAP Help Portal documentation.

The following picture shows an example oif a manually created Processing Variant:

 

 Step 6: Save as Draft

While working on your variant, you can save it as a draft. Draft variants are fully editable-you can change any configuration option, the Display ID, descriptions, or even the base Shipping Process.

Draft variants are not yet available for use in Transportation Requests or Consignments. Use the draft phase to validate your configuration before activation.

Step 7: Activate the Variant

Once you're satisfied with the configuration, activate the variant. This makes it available for:

  • Business rules that determine Processing Variants automatically
  • Manual selection when creating Transportation Requests
  • Assignment to Transportation Requests through APIs

Critical: After activation, the configuration options become immutable. You can still update descriptions and translations, but you cannot change which features are enabled or the base Shipping Process. This immutability ensures that active Transportation Requests and Consignments cannot be affected by configuration changes mid-process.

The Golden Rules of Processing Variant Maintenance

To ensure system stability and maintain trust in your logistics processes, Processing Variants follow strict governance rules:

Rule 1: Configuration Immutability After Activation

Once a Processing Variant is activated and referenced by any Transportation Request or Consignment, its configuration attributes are permanently locked. You cannot:

  • Change which Shipping Process it's based on
  • Enable or disable consolidation cutoff
  • Modify handling unit requirements
  • Alter any other process-controlling attributes

Why this restriction? Imagine you have 50 active Consignments using a variant configured for consolidation. If you could suddenly disable consolidation, those Consignments would be in an undefined state—the system wouldn't know whether to continue consolidating or stop. The immutability rule prevents such scenarios.

If you need to change a variant's configuration, create a new variant with your desired settings. Update your business rules to use the new variant for future Transportation Requests.

What You CAN Change After Activation:

  • Descriptions - Improve wording, add clarifications, fix typos
  • Default status - Mark a different variant as default

These changes don't affect process behavior for existing documents, so they're safe to modify at any time.

We already have ideas for a deprecation process for processing variants, but this will w´be introduced in a later step.

Rule 2: SAP-Managed Variants Are Completely Read-Only

The Processing Variants delivered by SAP (identifiable by Display IDs starting with "/SAP/") are completely read-only. You cannot modify:

  • Configuration options
  • Descriptions (including translations)
  • Display IDs
  • Any other attributes

SAP maintains these variants as tested, stable baselines. When SAP needs to update descriptions or add new translations, these changes come through system updates and migrations.

If an SAP-delivered variant almost meets your needs but requires one change, create a custom variant instead. Copy the concept, adjust the configuration, and give it your own Display ID.

Rule 3: Only One Default Variant

You can designate exactly one Processing Variant as the default. This variant serves as a fallback when:

  • Business rules cannot determine a specific variant
  • APIs create Transportation Requests without specifying a variant
  • Manual processes need a "safe" default choice

When you mark a variant as default using the "Set Default Processing Variant" action, the system automatically removes the default flag from whichever variant previously held it. There's no risk of accidentally having multiple defaults.

Choosing the Right Default:

Select a default variant that represents your most common or most flexible scenario. Typically, this means:

  • A variant without highly restrictive settings
  • One that applies to the broadest set of shipments
  • Your "standard" process that covers 70-80% of cases

The /SAP/No_Shipping variant is delivered as the default, but you'll likely want to change this to your primary shipping process variant once you've configured your business rules.

Setting the Default Processing Variant

To set a different variant as the default:

  1. Navigate to the Configure Shipping Processes app
  2. Select the variant you want to make default
  3. Click the "Set Default Processing Variant" action in the action toolbar
  4. The system updates the selected variant's default status and clears it from the previous default

The change takes effect immediately and applies to all future Transportation Requests where no specific variant is determined.

Troubleshooting Common Maintenance Scenarios

Scenario 1: "I need to change a configuration option on an active variant"

Solution: You cannot modify active variant configurations. Instead:

  1. Create a new custom variant with the desired configuration
  2. Give it a new Display ID (e.g., add "_V2" to indicate version 2)
  3. Update your business rules to use the new variant for future Transportation Requests
  4. Existing Transportation Requests continue using the old variant until completion
  5. Once all Transportation Requests using the old variant are complete, you can consider deprecating it

Scenario 2: "Which variant should I choose as the default?"

Solution: Analyze your Transportation Request creation patterns:

  • Which Shipping Process do you use most frequently?
  • What percentage of Transportation Requests can't be determined by business rules?
  • Which variant represents your "safest" fallback process?

For most customers, the default should be either /SAP/LTL or /SAP/Direct_LTL, depending on whether you typically consolidate shipments or handle them individually.

Scenario 3: "How do I know which variant a Transportation Request is using?"

Solution: The Processing Variant is displayed in the Transportation Request details. You can also filter Transportation Requests by Processing Variant in the Manage Transportation Requests app.

If you need to analyze variant usage at scale, the Processing Variant field is available in reporting and analytics.

Scenario 4: "Can I delete a Processing Variant that's no longer needed?"

Solution: Deletion of Processing Variants is not currently supported through the UI. Here's why:

  • Historical Transportation Requests reference the variant
  • Reporting and analytics need the variant definition
  • Audit trails require variant information

Instead of deletion, consider creating clear naming conventions that indicate deprecated variants (e.g., prefixing with "DEPRECATED_") and updating descriptions to indicate they should no longer be used.

Integration with Business Rules

While the Configure Shipping Processes app lets you create and maintain Processing Variants, the real power comes from integrating them with business rules for automatic determination.

In the Business Rules configuration, you can define rules like:

  • "For Transportation Requests from Shipping Point 1000 with weight < 500kg → use CUSTOM_LTL_CONSOLIDATION"
  • "For express orders → use CUSTOM_LTL_EXPRESS"

The Processing Variants you create in the Configure Shipping Processes app become available as targets in these business rules. This separation of concerns is powerful:

  • Processing Variants define what each process variation does
  • Business Rules define when each variant should be used

You can change business rules (when to use a variant) without affecting variant configurations (what the variant does).

Wrapping Up

The Configure Shipping Processes app puts powerful configuration capabilities at your fingertips, letting you tailor SAP Logistics Management to your exact business requirements without coding. By understanding the maintenance workflows, governance rules, and best practices, you can confidently create and manage Processing Variants that support your logistics operations.

Key takeaways for maintaining Processing Variants:

  • Start with SAP-delivered variants and only create custom ones when truly needed
  • Use clear, descriptive Display IDs and comprehensive descriptions
  • Remember that configurations are immutable after activation—test thoroughly first
  • Integrate with business rules for automatic, intelligent variant determination
  • Maintain documentation and review periodically to prevent configuration drift

Have you created custom Processing Variants in your LGM implementation? What configuration options would you like to see added in future releases? Share your experiences and suggestions in the comments!

entry.title: The New Geomap Control in MDC Library: Bring SAP Geomap Into Your UI5 Apps
entry.content:
Intro
We’re excited to announce the release of an experimental MDC control for displaying interactive maps — visualizing GeoJSON data, integrating vector and raster tiles from various providers, enabling features like clustering and selection, or handling user interaction — sap.ui.mdc.Geomap offers a native UI5 interface to achieve this without building custom wrappers.  

 

 

What’s in it for you  

  • Faster integration: Directly embed a production-grade map component into your UI5 applications, leveraging familiar UI5 properties, events, and aggregations.  
  • Flexible data & visualization: Seamlessly combine various tile providers (ESRI, MapBox, Azure maps, SAP HSS, etc.) with GeoJSON data sources. Manage diverse layers like fill, line, circle, heatmap, or symbol layers directly from your application’s model.  
  • Native UI5 behavior: Benefit from standard UI5 control lifecycle, data binding, and composition, ensuring the map integrates smoothly with your existing UI5 patterns.  
  • Build-friendly: This library is specifically designed for UI5 runtime consumption.  
  • SAP HANA Spatial Services (HSS) integration: Allows to run with data provided from HSS.

 

What’s included  

  • A new experimental control in MDC library:  sap.ui.mdc.Geomap  
  • Core building blocks: The main Geomap control, alongside specialized controls for providers, data sources, various layer types (such as, GeomapFillLayer, GeomapLineLayer, GeomapHeatmapLayer, GeomapSymbolLayer), and common map UI controls (such as, GeomapNavigationControl, GeomapScaleControl, GeomapSelectionControl, GeomapCopyrightControl).  
  • Full capabilities including: Rendering a dynamic, responsive map canvas with pan, zoom, rotate, and tilt capabilities. Supports smooth animations, full-screen mode, and adapts to all screen sizes, including mobile devices.  Built on WebGL for high-performance rendering. Map view can be programmatically controlled or manipulated by the user .

 

Multi-provider support  

Integrates with multiple map data providers for maximum flexibility. Out-of-the-box support for ESRI, HERE, OpenStreetMap and SAP HANA Spatial Services. Easily switch providers or configure custom styles and tokens.  

 

Rich layer system  

Visualize geospatial data using multiple layer types, each optimized for different visualization needs.  

  • Fill Layer: Colored polygons for regions, zones, or districts.  
  • Circle Layer: Point markers with customizable radius and color.  
  • Line Layer: Draw paths, routes, or connections.  
  • Heatmap Layer: Visualize data density or clustering.  
  • Symbol Layer: Display icons and text labels for feature identification.  

 

Advanced clustering  

  • Automatically groups nearby features to reduce clutter and improve performance.  
  • Dynamic cluster sizing, customizable properties, and click-through expansion. Especially useful for large datasets.  

  

Drawing & selection tools  

Enables users to select or annotate map features using lasso, circle, polygon or rectangle tools. Supports multi-feature selection.   

 

GeoJSON data support  

  •  Loads and visualizes standard GeoJSON data, including points, lines, polygons, and multi-geometries.  
  • Supports dynamic updates, feature properties, and optimized rendering for large datasets. Clustering available for point data.  

  

Comprehensive controls  

  •  Built-in UI controls for navigation, scale, fullscreen, copyright, and selection. 
  •  Controls can be positioned, enabled/disabled. 

  

Event-driven architecture  

Rich event system for application integration and interactive workflows. Listens for map lifecycle, user interaction, feature, drawing, and data update events.  

 

Styling & theming  

Professional, customizable styling with support for multiple color palettes, icons, and themes.  Data-driven styling rules, CSS variable integration, and dark/light theme support.  

 

Performance optimization  

Designed for enterprise-scale performance. Automatic tile-based rendering, lazy loading, efficient clustering, and WebGL acceleration.   

 

Internationalization  

Inclusive design with keyboard navigation, screen reader compatibility, and multi-language support. ARIA labels, high contrast mode, and full i18n support.  

   

How to use it  

The sap.ui.mdc.Geomap library is delivered as part of OpenUI5. It’s designed to be included and consumed through your standard UI5 build processes or runtime distribution.  

Link to samples: https://sapui5.hana.ondemand.com/#/entity/sap.ui.mdc.Geomap

MDC tutorial: https://github.com/SAP-samples/ui5-mdc-json-tutorial/tree/main/u2/ex1

 

Get started  

As an experimental library, OpenUI5 Geomap is in its initial stages, and its API is not yet finalized. This presents a unique opportunity for you to contribute to its evolution by providing feedback and suggestions. Be aware that some APIs may change or be removed in future updates.   

We are excited to see the innovative applications you will create using the SAPUI5 Geomap library.   

Happy geocoding!

entry.title: Re: U.S SECURE 2.0 Act - Section 603
entry.content:
Hello @James297009,

Thank you for reaching out. As you mentioned, the creation of /TB6 is essential to trigger the Section 603 rollover option. If /TB6 was not generated this is the root cause of your issue. Below are the responses to your questions:

  • Did you have to trigger /TB6 manually somehow? No, the /TB6 wage type is automatically generated in the first payroll period of the year.

  • Did you have to run a retro into December? This is not necessary. The system will read TCRT values of the prior year to create /TB6, even if a retro isn’t performed.

  • Did payroll only pick up /TB6 when the prior year’s results existed in TCRT? Yes, that is correct. To create /TB6, the values of wage type /703 must exist in the TCRT table of the prior year. If no prior year payroll results are present, you can generate /TB6 via /TB7. However, for this scenario, it would be necessary to exceed the 150k threshold in /TB7 to trigger the rollover.

  • Were there additional steps needed beyond what the note describes? All necessary steps are detailed in the Notes.

If you have followed the necessary steps and the rollover is still not occurring, please open a case with PA-PA-US-BN so that we can review the situation.

We would like to understand which APIs should be used as replacements for the following PPD services:

  2. Retrieve Routings – Outdated 
  3. Read_NC_CODE – Outdated

Thank you for your assistance.

entry.title: Congratulatory leave quota entitlement for different dependents
entry.content:
Hi Experts, 

Congratulatory leave if utilized for the first time, employee cannot apply for second occasion under this leave due to quota provided for one time as per calendar year basis.

So basically this is what we are trying to achieve. 

  1. If an employee has two children, they will be entitled to 4 working days (WD) of Congratulatory Leave (Marriage of Child), with 2 WD per child and be able to apply for it within the same year. 
  2. The child must be single. If the child is divorced, the employee will not be entitled to the quota for that child. 
  3. If the employee has fully utilized the quota and all children are married, no additional quota will be generated. 
  4. This applies only to legal (biological) children and is not applicable to other types of dependents. YES
  5. The quota for the following year will be generated based on the child’s marital status (Single).

Example: If an employee has 3 children and 2 of them got married in 2025, the quota for 2026 will be 2 WD, as only one child remains unmarried. 

I have updated the rules, but didnt work. Any idea to update the rules?

Thanks.

entry.title: Re: Following messages raised while executing migration o...
entry.content:
Hello @Fabiane_Melo74

Yes, there is a way but that will depend on what was posted.

To specifically answer your questions:

  • Is there a solution for me to reload the template with the correct values and have it account for them?
    • Yes, but typically via new project/new run, not by overwriting the finalised posted run.
  • Do I have to open another migration project in order to be able to execute the template?
    • In practice, yes for final-stage successful posted migrations.

Here are some details to consume:

  • If posting migration object is already completed and documents were posted in final migration mode, then you generally cannot re-simulate and overwrite the same posted run-in place.
  • For you to load corrected values, I suggest you create a new migration project or a new object run, reload the corrected file and post the correction properly.

Something you should consider before making your decision:

  1. If you had already posted wrong value to accounting, you need business correction postings and then load the corrected values as you may require. Do not try to replace already posted migration documents directly.
  2. If your system did not record any accounting impact despite objects being technically completed, you may use a new migration project for clean reprocessing.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: How to Get Started with Pipeline for Cloud Integration
entry.content:
This blog is a beginner’s blog for getting started with Pipeline for Cloud Integration, for integration developers having experience in Cloud Integration in general. The Pipeline was introduced beginning of 2024, when it was a concept (referred to as Pipeline Concept) as part of the migration guide for migrating from SAP Process Orchestration / SAP Process Integration to SAP Integration Suite. In the last months and years, the Pipeline has been evolved from a concept to a solution, being regularly enhanced with new features and customer requirements. That’s why there is a lot of Pipeline-related content available. As a central starting point, this blog aims to shed light on what the Pipeline is about and how to initially set it up in Cloud Integration.

 

  • Motivation
  • Use Cases
  • Architecture
  • Initial Setup of the Pipeline
  • Point-to-point scenario (sample 5)
  • Copy standard packages from SAP Business Accelerator Hub
  • Deploy generic Integration Flows
  • Create and deploy scenario-specific Integration Flows
  • Setup Partner Directory entries
  • Run your first scenario
  • Scenario with XSLT for content-based routing (sample 1A)
  • Copy standard packages from SAP Business Accelerator Hub
  • Deploy generic Integration Flows
  • Create and deploy scenario-specific Integration Flows
  • Setup Partner Directory entries
  • Run your second scenario
  • Accelerator Tool
  • Link Collection

 

Motivation

The Pipeline is a framework designed to ease setting up integration scenarios. Instead of starting from scratch each time, it uses a standard design provided by SAP. This saves time and reduces complexity because you can reuse the same building blocks for different scenarios. It works by handling tasks asynchronously, which helps keep systems running smoothly. The Pipeline offers strong error-handling and retry options, both automatic and manual, to keep messages flowing. Plus, it allows content-based routing based on the message content during processing. It ensures that messages are processed Exactly Once and supports idempotency, keeping data accurate and consistent.

The Pipeline simplifies complex integration setups, like content-based routing and recipient lists, and helps in cases of receivers and interfaces splits. Generic integration flows can be reused across scenarios, cutting down on effort and complexity. It works well for the migration of SAP PI/PO to Cloud Integration, with support from the migration tool, while keeping necessary processing features intact. You can also use the Pipeline to create new scenarios, whether they are asynchronous or synchronous. It efficiently uses limited resources in Cloud Integration, like JMS queues, by sharing them across several scenarios. By using the standard, this framework is flexible, allowing for customization and extension to fit specific use cases.

 

Use Cases

The Pipeline for Cloud Integration is suited for but not primarily restricted to companies transitioning from SAP PI/PO to Cloud Integration. It provides a smooth migration process leveraging the migration tool. Beyond migrations, the Pipeline is a solution for designing new integration scenarios in a uniform structure, making it easier for maintenance and monitoring. It can use generic inbound integration flows as single entry points for both XI proxy and IDoc scenarios, streamlining message processing, and has an option for generic XI or IDoc outbound flows. Additionally, the Pipeline supports extended receiver determination, enabling more precise routing than usual XSLT mappings.

For simpler uses, point-to-point scenarios are supported, where you can bypass receiver and interface determination steps for faster runtime and simpler configuration, relying on string parameters from the Partner Directory instead of XSLT mappings. For special situations, the Pipeline supports the use of receiver-specific JMS queues, although the default is a generic outbound queue to keep operations straightforward. It also accommodates combined receiver and interface determinations in a single XSLT, as well as sender wildcard scenarios. Altogether, the Pipeline allows to stay in standard while supporting a variety of use cases.

 

Architecture

The Pipeline works with multiple Integration Flows using a limited number of JMS queues for asynchronous scenarios. There are generic and scenario-specific Integration Flows: generic Integration Flows are developed and enhanced by SAP, being published in SAP Business Accelerator Hub (see next chapter “Initial Setup of Pipeline”). The scenario-specific Integration Flows do not contain lots of complexity and are either generated by the migration tool, or you can create them manually. For different scenarios, messages share the generic Integration Flows as well as the JMS queues – the latter one is important as it targets the reuse of limited resources in Cloud Integration.

For content-based routing and dynamic message processing behavior, the Pipeline relies on the Partner Directory, from which it reads the required information at runtime. Based on the sender system and the sender interface, the Pipeline looks up the Partner ID (Pid) in the Partner Directory. The Pid is the name of your integration scenario, which holds together the scenario, containing string parameters for dynamic message processing behavior as well as receiver and interface determination, either as binary parameters for content-based routing using XSLT mappings, or as string parameters for point-to-point scenarios (which have exactly one receiver system and one receiver interface).

The architectural diagram almost shows the bare minimum required for Cloud Integration: 4 Integration Flows (2 generic Integration Flows, 2 scenario-specific Integration Flows), 2 JMS queues, and the Partner Directory (with minimum 2 entries per scenario being 1 alternative partner and 1 binary parameter for content-based routing with combined receiver determination). From this architecture, the Pipeline can be highly extended with more Integration Flows, JMS queues, and Partner Directory entries, to implement a variety of different scenarios and use cases by using the standard framework.

 

Initial Setup of the Pipeline

To set up the Pipeline for the first time, you can follow the steps below to run your first working scenarios. In this blog post, two scenarios are described with a step-by-step guide which you can follow along to set up the Pipeline in your tenant. You can use a development tenant or even setup a trial tenant to follow along.

 

Point-to-point scenario (sample 5)

From our experience, most of the integration scenarios are point-to-point, meaning that, for a combination of sender system and sender interface, these scenarios have exactly one receiver system with one receiver interface. These scenarios bypass the content-based routing and therefore are easier to setup by using string parameters in the Partner Directory for receiver and interface determination.

The following example is sample scenario 5 (PID: PIP_Samples_Scenario_5) of the sample scenarios by @alex_bundschuh. As explained above, you need 4 Integration Flows for that, as well as 2 (automatically created) JMS queues and some Partner Directory entries. The following table shows how this fits together (in order of occurrence at runtime):

Type

Technical name in standard

Name in picture of architecture

Purpose

Integration Flow scenario-specific

PIPSamplesScenario5_Step01
(custom name)

Inbound (scenario-specific)

This Integration Flow receives the incoming message and puts it to the generic JMS queue for decoupling.

JMS queue

PIPX01

Inbound X queue

The JMS queue holds the message until it is taken by the next Integration Flow and eventually handles retries.

Integration Flow generic

Pipeline v2 Generic Step02 – Integrated Messaging Runtime Async

Integrated Messaging Async (generic)

After looking up the Pid from the Partner Directory, this Integration Flow covers both receiver and interface determination, covering special cases such as point-to-point scenarios.

Partner Directory entry – alternative partner

PIP_Samples_Scenario_5

Partner Directory

By looking up the combination of sender system and sender interface, the PID is determined as name of the scenario.

Partner Directory entry – string parameter

PIP_Samples_Scenario_5

Partner Directory

For the PID, this entry holds the string parameters needed for point-to-point receiver and interface determination.

JMS queue

PIPQ04

Outbound queue

The JMS queue holds the message until it is taken by the next Integration Flow and eventually handles retries.

Integration Flow generic

Pipeline v2 Generic Step06 – Outbound Processing

Outbound (generic)

This Integration Flow prepares the message to be sent out and calls the scenario-specific ProcessDirect address.

Integration Flow scenario-specific

PIPSamplesScenario5_Step07_RCV1_IF1
(custom name)

Outbound (scenario-specific)

This Integration Flow is called via ProcessDirect adapter. If necessary, scenario-specific conversion can be done here for the receiver system, before the message is sent out of Cloud Integration.

 

You might be wondering why the Integration Flows cover step01, step02, step06, and step07. This is due to historical reasons, steps 02, 04, and 05 are now all covered by Integration Flow “Pipeline v2 Generic Step02 – Integrated Messaging Runtime Async” and step03 is an optional scenario-specific Integration Flow if inbound conversion like JSON to XML or CSV to XML is needed (the latter one is shown in the picture of architecture with the name “Inbound Conversion (scenario-specific)”). With that, let’s start step by step with setting up the Pipeline in your tenant.

 

Copy standard packages from SAP Business Accelerator Hub

In total, there are four active packages related to the Pipeline. For the initial setup, however, you only need to copy two of these packages to your Cloud Integration tenant:

  • Cloud Integration Pipeline – Generic Pipelines
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline – Generic Outbound

For that, find the packages in the “Discover > Integrations” area of your tenant and simply copy both packages to the tenant.

Deploy generic Integration Flows

From these packages, only two Integration Flows need to be deployed to get started.

  • Pipeline v2 Generic Step02 – Integrated Messaging Runtime Async from package “Cloud Integration Pipeline – Generic Pipelines”
  • Pipeline v2 Generic Step06 – Outbound Processing from package “Cloud Integration Pipeline – Generic Outbound”

This can be done in the “Design > Integrations and APIs” area of the same tenant. The default configuration of the Integration Flows is sufficient; you can just deploy them both without any changes.

Once these Integration Flows are deployed, you can check which JMS queues have been created in the background. For that, go to “Monitor > Integrations and APIs > Message Queues”. The default queues are “PIPX01” and “PIPQ04”, both come with a dead letter queue “PIPX01_DLQ” and “PIPQ04_DLQ” in which the message ends up if the number of retries have been exceeded. (Additionally, you might see “PIPQ01”, which used to be the old default queue. When using Integration Flow “Pipeline v2 Generic Step02 – Integrated Messaging Runtime Async”, the default queue is “PIPX01”.)

 

Create and deploy scenario-specific Integration Flows

Generally, these scenario-specific Integration Flows would either be generated by the migration tool, or you would copy them from the templates package, or you would create them manually, as they usually don’t contain too much logic.

For this sample, you can import the sample package for scenario 5 to your tenant. In “Design > Integrations and APIs”, import the package from your local device. Without any modification needed, deploy the following artifacts:

  • PIPSamplesScenario5_Step01
  • PIPSamplesScenario5_Step07_RCV1_IF1

Additionally, there is a little helpers package which you can import to your tenant to deploy this Integration Flow simulating a receiver system:

  • PIP_Mocked_Receiver

 

Setup Partner Directory entries

To setup the Partner Directory entries required for your first scenario, you can use the Partner Directory UI. To access it, select “Monitor > Integrations and APIs > Partner Directory” in your tenant. In addition, there is a Pipeline-specific accelerator tool which helps you to set up your Partner Directory entries. You can learn more about this tool in the upcoming chapter.

You’ll see that all Partner Directory entries (no matter if it’s alternative partners, string parameters, or binary parameters) have the same partner ID, which holds together the scenario. For a point-to-point scenario, we need two string parameters for receiver and interface determination, all others (like MaxJMSRetries in that case) are optional and can be used to influence the message processing behavior at runtime.

Alternative partner:

{
    "Pid": "PIP_Samples_Scenario_5",
    "Agency": "Sender_51",
    "Scheme": "SenderInterface",
    "Id": "SalesOrder.Create"
}

String parameters:

{
    "Pid": "PIP_Samples_Scenario_5",
    "Id": "MaxJMSRetries",
    "Value": "2"
}
{
    "Pid": "PIP_Samples_Scenario_5",
    "Id": "receiverDetermination",
    "Value": "Receiver_51"
}
{
    "Pid": "PIP_Samples_Scenario_5",
    "Id": "interfaceDetermination_Receiver_51",
    "Value": "/PIP/Step07/Samples/Scenario_5/Receiver_51/IfIdx1"
}

 

Run your first scenario

With this setup, you’re able to run your first scenario through Pipeline for Cloud Integration. For that, prepare an HTTP message in the HTTP client of your choice, e.g. Postman or Bruno.

### trigger sample scenario 5
POST https://{{host}}/http/PIP/Step01/Samples/Scenario_5
Authorization: usual authentication to call an Integration Flow, e.g. basic authentication or OAuth using a service key from SAP BTP
Content-Type: application/xml
SAP_Sender: Sender_51
SAP_SenderInterface: SalesOrder.Create
xPipeline: true

<soapenv:Envelope xmlns:soapenv="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/" xmlns:pi="http://pi-elevation.bootcamp.com">
    <soapenv:Header/>
    <soapenv:Body>
        <pi:MT_SalesOrder_0001>
            <salesOrder>
                <orderNumber>50001</orderNumber>
                <orderDate>10/01/2023</orderDate>
                <customer>
                    <firstName>Amruta</firstName>
                    <lastName>Kamble</lastName>
                    <street>Hauptstrasse</street>
                    <number>89</number>
                </customer>
                <items>
                    <itemNumber>Test</itemNumber>
                    <material>Test_material</material>
                    <quantity>5</quantity>
                    <price>550</price>
                </items>
            </salesOrder>
        </pi:MT_SalesOrder_0001>
    </soapenv:Body>
</soapenv:Envelope>

Under “Monitor > Integrations and APIs > Monitor Message Processing”, you can check how your message was processed at runtime. There, you should see 5 new messages with status “Completed”. They all have the same correlation ID; you can filter by this correlation ID to only display the messages corresponding to the last run of your scenario. With that, you can see that the steps 01, 02, 06, and 07 of the Pipeline were called. At runtime, your Partner Directory entries have been accessed by step02. For each entry, there are details on the right-hand side such as sender and receiver as well as custom headers set by the Pipeline.

Congratulation, your first scenario is running! This is almost the simplest option of running the Pipeline, yet it is heavily used in practice. To build on that, you can take a look at the next scenario using content-based routing. Since you have now initially set up the pipeline, new scenarios will be set up with even fewer steps.

 

Scenario with XSLT for content-based routing (sample 1A)

The following example is sample scenario 1A (PID: PIP_Samples_Scenario_1A) of the sample scenarios by @alex_bundschuh. As explained above, you need 4 Integration Flows for that, as well as 2 (automatically created) JMS queues and some Partner Directory entries. The following table shows how this fits together (in order of occurrence at runtime):

Type

Technical name in standard

Name in picture of architecture

Purpose

Integration Flow scenario-specific

PIPSamplesScenario1_Step01
(custom name)

Inbound (scenario-specific)

This Integration Flow receives the incoming message and puts it to the generic JMS queue for decoupling.

JMS queue

PIPX01

Inbound X queue

The JMS queue holds the message until it is taken by the next Integration Flow and eventually handles retries.

Integration Flow generic

Pipeline v2 Generic Step02 – Integrated Messaging Runtime Async

Integrated Messaging Async (generic)

After looking up the Pid from the Partner Directory, this Integration Flow covers both receiver and interface determination, covering special cases such as point-to-point scenarios.

Partner Directory entry – alternative partner

PIP_Samples_Scenario_1A

Partner Directory

By looking up the combination of sender system and sender interface, the PID is determined as name of the scenario.

Partner Directory entry – string parameter

PIP_Samples_Scenario_1A

Partner Directory

Based on the Pid, the Pipeline checks if a strung parameter with Id InboundConversionEndpoint is set in the Partner Directory, which is done for this scenario.

Integration Flow scenario-specific

PIPSamplesScenario1_Step03 (custom name)

Inbound conversion (scenario-specific)

Based on the ProcessDirect address set in the Partner Directory, a custom Integration Flow is called for inbound conversion. In this scenario, this is done by a JSON to XML converter.

Partner Directory entry – binary parameter

PIP_Samples_Scenario_1A

Partner Directory

For the PID, this entry holds the corresponding XSLT mapping for content-based routing.

JMS queue

PIPQ04

Outbound queue

The JMS queue holds the message until it is taken by the next Integration Flow and eventually handles retries.

Integration Flow generic

Pipeline v2 Generic Step06 – Outbound Processing

Outbound (generic)

This Integration Flow prepares the message to be sent out and calls the scenario-specific ProcessDirect address.

Integration Flow scenario-specific

PIPSamplesScenario1_Step07_RCVx_IFx
(custom name)

Outbound (scenario-specific)

This Integration Flow is called via ProcessDirect adapter. If necessary, scenario-specific conversion can be done here for the receiver system, before the message is sent out of Cloud Integration.

 

Copy standard packages from SAP Business Accelerator Hub

As these packages are reused and we have already done this for our first scenario, we don’t need to do anything in this step.

 

Deploy generic Integration Flows

These are the same Integration Flows which we have deployed earlier for the first scenario, so no action required for now.

 

Create and deploy scenario-specific Integration Flows

For this sample, you can import the sample package for scenario 1A to your tenant. In “Design > Integrations and APIs”, import the package from your local device. Without any modification needed, deploy the following artifacts (in this scenario, step07 relies on a message mapping, which also needs to be deployed):

  • PIPSamplesScenario1_Step01
  • PIPSamplesScenario1_Step03
  • PIPSamplesScenario1_Step07_RCV2_IF1
  • mm_item_to_order

 

Setup Partner Directory entries

This time, for content-based routing, we need an XSLT as binary parameter for receiver and interface determination in addition to alternative partners and string parameters. These entries can again be created in the Partner Directory UI or with the accelerator tool.

Alternative partner:

{
    "Pid": "PIP_Samples_Scenario_1A",
    "Agency": "Sender_1A",
    "Scheme": "SenderInterface",
    "Id": "PurchaseOrderItem.Create"
}

String parameters:

{
    "Pid": "PIP_Samples_Scenario_1A",
    "Id": "MaxJMSRetries",
    "Value": "1"
}
{
    "Pid": "PIP_Samples_Scenario_1A",
    "Id": "InboundConversionEndpoint",
    "Value": "/PIP/Step03/Samples/Scenario_1"
}

Binary parameters:

You can save the following XSLT as .xsl file on your local device and then upload it in the Partner Directory UI. Especially when dealing with Binary parameters, the recommendation is to use the accelerator tool, as this aims to simplify your interaction with the Partner Directory for the Pipeline. Using this tool as described in the chapter below, you don’t have to create the XSLT from scratch on your own, but the tool can generate it for your based on a tabular input – make sure to check the tool out!

{
    "Pid": "PIP_Samples_Scenario_1A",
    "Id": "receiverDetermination"
    "ContentType": "xsl",
    "Value": upload the following XSLT from your local device
}
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<xsl:stylesheet xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform" version="3.0">
<xsl:param name="dc_country" />
<xsl:template match="/">
        <ns0:Receivers xmlns:ns0="http://sap.com/xi/XI/System">
            <ReceiverNotDetermined>
                <Type>Error</Type>
                <DefaultReceiver />
            </ReceiverNotDetermined>
            <xsl:if test="/*:Item/Category = 'Keyboards'">
                <Receiver>
                    <Service>Receiver_11</Service>
                    <Interfaces>
                        <xsl:if test="/*:Item/Quantity = '10'">
                            <Interface>
                                <Index>1</Index>
                                <Service>/PIP/Step07/Samples/Scenario_1/Receiver_11/IfIdx1</Service>
                            </Interface>
                        </xsl:if>
                        <xsl:if test="/*:Item/CurrencyCode = 'EUR'">
                            <Interface>
                                <Index>2</Index>
                                <Service>/PIP/Step07/Samples/Scenario_1/Receiver_11/IfIdx2</Service>
                            </Interface>
                        </xsl:if>
                        <xsl:if test="/*:Item/Quantity != '10'">
                            <Interface>
                                <Index>3</Index>
                                <Service>/PIP/Step07/Samples/Scenario_1/Receiver_11/IfIdx3</Service>
                            </Interface>
                        </xsl:if>
                    </Interfaces>
                </Receiver>
            </xsl:if>
            <xsl:if test="(/*:Item/Category = 'Keyboards') or (/*:Item/Category = 'Software')">
                <Receiver>
                    <Service>Receiver_12</Service>
                    <Interfaces>
                        <xsl:if test="$dc_country = 'DE'">
                            <Interface>
                                <Index>1</Index>
                                <Service>/PIP/Step07/Samples/Scenario_1/Receiver_12/IfIdx1</Service>
                            </Interface>
                        </xsl:if>
                        <xsl:if test="$dc_country != 'DE'">
                            <Interface>
                                <Index>2</Index>
                                <Service>/PIP/Step07/Samples/Scenario_1/Receiver_12/IfIdx2</Service>
                            </Interface>
                        </xsl:if>
                    </Interfaces>
                </Receiver>
            </xsl:if>
            <xsl:if test="(/*:Item/Category = 'Keyboards') and (/*:Item/ProductName != 'XXXX')">
                <Receiver>
                    <Service>Receiver_13</Service>
                    <Interfaces>
                        <xsl:if test="/*:Item/Quantity = '10'">
                            <Interface>
                                <Index>1</Index>
                                <Service>/PIP/Step07/Samples/Scenario_1/Receiver_13/IfIdx1</Service>
                            </Interface>
                        </xsl:if>
                        <xsl:if test="/*:Item/CurrencyCode = 'EUR'">
                            <Interface>
                                <Index>2</Index>
                                <Service>/PIP/Step07/Samples/Scenario_1/Receiver_13/IfIdx2</Service>
                            </Interface>
                        </xsl:if>
                        <xsl:if test="/*:Item/Quantity != '10'">
                            <Interface>
                                <Index>3</Index>
                                <Service>/PIP/Step07/Samples/Scenario_1/Receiver_13/IfIdx3</Service>
                            </Interface>
                        </xsl:if>
                    </Interfaces>
                </Receiver>
            </xsl:if>
        </ns0:Receivers>
    </xsl:template>
</xsl:stylesheet>

Alternatively, in the accelerator tool, your scenario would look like below. Without the need to deal with the syntax of the XSLT, you can focus on entering the XPath conditions and receiver system / receiver interface, to generate the XSLT from your input. For more details on this accelerator tool, please find the next chapter.

 

Run your second scenario

### trigger sample scenario 1A
POST https://{{host}}/http/PIP/Step01/Samples/Scenario_1?country=DE
Authorization: usual authentication to call an Integration Flow, e.g. basic authentication or OAuth using a service key from SAP BTP
Content-Type: application/json
SAP_Sender: Sender_1A
SAP_SenderInterface: PurchaseOrderItem.Create
xPipeline: true

{
    "ns0:Item":{
        "@PurchaseOrderNumber":"1A9201",
        "@OrderDate":"2019-05-06",
        "@ItemNumber":"10",
        "ProductId":"HT-XXXX",
        "ProductName":"XXXX",
        "Category":"Software",
        "Quantity":"10",
        "CurrencyCode":"EUR",
        "Price":"799.00"
    }
}

Under “Monitor > Integrations and APIs > Monitor Message Processing”, you can again check how your message was processed at runtime, ideally filtered by the correlation ID. This time, step03 has also be called for scenario-specific inbound conversion, as this is set as a string parameter in the Partner Directory.

With that, also your second scenario is running! This is just the beginning – there are lots of more options and sample scenarios available. You can easily set them up with a Postman collection provided – there are different messages you can test for each scenario, e.g., for scenario 1A, there is a message prepared where you can observe receivers and interfaces splits.

When further discovering the Pipeline, our strong recommendation is to not edit the generic Integration Flows but stay in the standard to easily get updates. Most of the use cases can be implemented with the standard, as the Pipeline keeps evolving covering more and more different scenarios.

 

Accelerator Tool

As you saw during the initial setup, we deal with XSLT mappings for content-based routing. To make maintenance of XSLT, replication of Partner Directory content, and other topics related to Partner Directory easier, there is an accelerator tool specifically developed for Pipeline for Cloud Integration. The idea is to use this SAP open source tool in combination with our standard UI. Feel free to give it a try, there is also a session available from Devtoberfest 2025 which shows the setup step by step.

 

Link Collection

SAP Help

Standard documentation as part of migration guide from SAP PO: https://help.sap.com/docs/migration-guide-po/migration-guide-for-sap-process-orchestration/pipeline-concept

Partner Directory entries for the Pipeline: https://help.sap.com/docs/migration-guide-po/migration-guide-for-sap-process-orchestration/using-partner-directory-in-pipeline-concept

Partner Directory UI: https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration/managing-partner-directory-entries

 

Introduction of the Pipeline at Devtoberfest: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LFfW8rnm4fU

 

SAP Community (recent blogs after package split)

Version 1.0.0: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/new-integration-packages-for-the-pipeline-for-cloud-integration/ba-p/14175339

  • Package split
  • Custom preprocessing exit: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/new-feature-for-the-pipeline-for-cloud-integration-custom-preprocessing/ba-p/14199571
  • New templates for enhancing the custom receiver determination: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/new-templates-for-the-pipeline-for-cloud-integration-enhancing-the-custom/ba-p/14199651

Version 1.0.1: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/introducing-a-new-version-1-0-1-of-the-pipeline-for-cloud-integration/ba-p/14257263

  • We now support a generic IDoc outbound flow to standardize and ease the configuration of IDoc outbound scenarios: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/generic-idoc-outbound-flow-in-cloud-integration-pipeline/ba-p/14269062
  • We now support a generic XI outbound flow to standardize and ease the configuration of XI outbound scenarios: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/generic-xi-outbound-flow-in-cloud-integration-pipeline/ba-p/14269061
  • We have improved the support for sender and receiver parties.
  • We have improved the support for receiver-specific queues and the definition of the queue for receiver-specific outbound processing.
  • In a sender wildcard scenario, we now support inbound conversion per sender.
  • Non-XML payloads are encoded before storing in an exchange property to avoid that the payload gets corrupted when reading from the exchange property again.
  • For some scenarios, you have the option to run scenarios in the pipeline without the need of the Partner Directory: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/run-pipeline-for-cloud-integration-without-the-partner-directory/ba-p/14257311

December 2025 shipment: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/new-fixes-and-features-in-the-pipeline-for-cloud-integration-package-with/ba-p/14294379

  • We have changed the duplicate handling for the generic XI and IDoc inbound processing flows as follows: In case of duplicates, the message processing logs end with Completed instead of Escalated to avoid that an http error code 500 is returned to the sending system.
  • For testing purposes, you can now switch off the duplicate check for the generic IDoc inbound processing flow. By default, the duplicate handling is enabled.
  • The XI inbound processing flow now supports dynamic adapter-specific headers. A feature which has just recently be shipped with an enhancement of the XI adapter. See Dynamic Headers.
  • An Exactly Once In Order (EOIO) check has been added to the IDoc inbound processing flow. Since we do not support EOIO in the Pipeline yet, the message goes into an error.
  • The definition of alternative partners has been extended to be able to determine partner ID based on sender component name, sender interface name, and sender interface namespace. This was supported before for XI and IDoc inbound scenarios, now you can do this for any scenario if required. If not, you can stick to the option to determine partner ID based on sender component name and sender interface name only.
  • We fixed an issue with a wrong reference to a script in the Pipeline v2 Generic Step02 - Integrated Messaging Runtime Sync flow.
  • The allowed headers list of the template flow Pipeline v2 Template - Custom Exit has been extended to support the header SapQualityOfService. This way, you have the option to use or change the SapQualityOfService header in a custom pre-processing flow. For instance, you do not like to change the EOIO setup of an XI interface in the sender system but still like to process the messages in the Pipeline accepting EO delivery as long as the Pipeline doesn't support EOIO. So you can call the custom exit flow right at the beginning of the generic XI inbound flow and switch from ExactlyOnceInOrder to ExactlyOnce.
  • A new template Pipeline v2 Template Step07 - Outbound Processing Point-to-Point to Generic XI Outbound has been added that allows you to create a scenario-specific outbound processing flow that calls the generic XI outbound processing flow. The template can be used for both synchronous and asynchronous communication.
  • A new template Pipeline v2 Template Step07 - Outbound Processing Point-to-Point to Generic IDoc Outbound has been added that allows you to create a scenario-specific outbound processing flow that calls the generic IDoc outbound processing flow.

 

SAP Business Accelerator Hub

Package “Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Pipelines”: https://hub.sap.com/package/CloudIntegrationPipelineGenericPipelines/integrationflow

  • Package for step02 (including integration messaging runtime async and sync), as well as old step02, step04, and step05
  • Integration flow “Pipeline v2 Generic Step02 - Integrated Messaging Runtime Async”: https://hub.sap.com/integrationflow/com.sap.integration.cloud.pipeline.v2.generic.step02.integrated.messaging.async

Package “Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Outbound”. https://hub.sap.com/package/CloudIntegrationPipelineGenericOutbound/integrationflow

  • Package for step06 and step08 (latter ones are optional for generic XI or IDoc outbound)
  • Integration flow “Pipeline v2 Generic Step06 - Outbound Processing”: https://hub.sap.com/integrationflow/com.sap.integration.cloud.pipeline.v2.generic.step06.outbound.processing

Package “Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound”: https://hub.sap.com/package/CloudIntegrationPipelineGenericInbound/integrationflow

  • Package for generic XI or IDoc entry point (optional)

Package “Cloud Integration Pipeline – Templates”: https://hub.sap.com/package/CloudIntegrationPipelineTemplates/integrationflow

  • Package with different Integration Flows for different steps and scenarios which can be customized based on your needs

 

Data Store Extension

Version 1.0.0: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/process-integration-pipeline-extension-restart-via-data-store/ba-p/14153116

Version 1.0.1: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/new-ci-pipeline-restart-extension-features-supporting-new-pipeline-package/ba-p/14230547

  • Supports new integration packages for the Pipeline for Cloud Integration and allows flexible linkage of Generic Pipeline copies to the Pipeline Stages.
  • Enhanced Restart Job Integration Flow:
  • Generic Job Profile to restart all integration scenarios defined in the Partner Directory through the Pipeline Framework.
  • New configuration options to persist the Job Log.
  • New Integration Flow "Pipeline API - Restart Job Log" to retrieve both the Restart Job run list and detailed log data (if log persistence is enabled).
  • Improved configuration and monitoring: New parameters (such as "NotOlderThanHours" for Restart Job), several bug fixes, enriched monitoring headers, and better exception handling.

 

Accelerator Tool

GitHub: https://github.com/SAP-samples/integration-suite-partner-directory-accelerator-for-pipeline-concept

Blog: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/partner-directory-accelerator-tool-for-pipeline-concept/ba-p/14095019

Devtoberfest: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m51z53fc1c4

 

History – older SAP Community blog posts before package split

Version 1.0.0 – initial release in March 2024: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/introducing-the-new-pipeline-concept-in-cloud-integration/ba-p/13639651

  • Applying the pipeline concept for an integration scenario in general: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/applying-the-pipeline-concept-for-an-integration-scenario-in-general/ba-p/13640018
  • Applying the pipeline concept for a multicast integration scenario: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/applying-the-pipeline-concept-for-a-multicast-integration-scenario/ba-p/13641417
  • Applying the pipeline concept for a point-to-point integration scenario: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/applying-the-pipeline-concept-for-a-point-to-point-integration-scenario/ba-p/13641474
  • Applying the pipeline concept for an interface split scenario with one receiver message type: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/applying-the-pipeline-concept-for-an-interface-split-scenario-with-one/ba-p/13641560
  • Applying the pipeline concept for an interface split scenario with multiple receiver message types: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/applying-the-pipeline-concept-for-an-interface-split-scenario-with-multiple/ba-p/13641662
  • Applying the pipeline concept for an interface split scenario with order at runtime flag set: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/applying-the-pipeline-concept-for-an-interface-split-scenario-with-order-at/ba-p/13641691

Version 1.0.5: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/latest-pipeline-concept-enhancements-custom-error-handling-and-others/ba-p/13757664

  • Extension for custom error handling
  • In generic IDoc inbound processing flow, display list of IDoc numbers in case of IDoc bulk messages
  • For IDoc inbound, sender interface is calculated as a combination of MESTYP, IDOCTYP and CIMTYP
  • Supporting test mode via testMode header in the allowed headers configuration
  • In standard error handling, copied messages from the dead letter queue are switched to DEBUG log level

Version 1.0.6: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/new-pipeline-concept-features-new-partner-id-definition-alternative-partner/ba-p/13787400

  • Revised partner id approach due to lengths restrictions of the partner id
  • Option to use alternative partner in case of sender wildcard scenarios and to overcome partner id restrictions
  • Changed default max retry to 5 instead of unlimited retry
  • For messages in test mode, failed messages are not put into retry
  • For generic integration flows, adapter parameters were externalized
  • Option to configure message end event type in case of message stored in dead letter queue, default is Escalated
  • Option to bypass receiver determination and interface determination steps in case of Point-to-Point pattern
  • Option to configure a pipeline JMS queue prefix which makes configuration of queue names easier if you create another set of generic integration flow

Version 1.0.7: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/new-pipeline-concept-features-supporting-custom-header-properties-and/ba-p/13894048

  • Support for custom header properties
  • Support for dynamic configuration parameters

Version 1.0.8: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/new-pipeline-concept-features-more-bypass-options-and-custom-extensions/ba-p/13955138

  • Option to combine receiver and interface determination in one single XSLT mapping for an improved runtime behavior
  • Option to bypass interface determination pipeline step in case of Recipient List pattern without interface split
  • Option to bypass receiver determination pipeline step in case of interface split scenarios with one single receiver only
  • Supporting header-based XSLT routing conditions if message body is non-XML
  • Extension for custom receiver determination instead of XSLT from Partner Directory
  • Extension for custom interface determination instead of XSLT from Partner Directory
  • New pipeline processing log providing more details if message is stored to the dead letter queue
  • Fixed standard retry handling for generic integration flows with splitter to avoid message duplicates

Version 1.0.9: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/new-pipeline-concept-features-integrated-runtimes-and-supporting-landscape/ba-p/14000234

  • Supporting different tenant stages, e.g., DEV, QA, PRD
  • New integrated messaging runtime "Pipeline Generic Step02 - Integrated Messaging Runtime Async" combining inbound conversion and receiver/interface determination in one pipeline step
  • New integrated messaging runtime "Pipeline Generic Step02 - Integrated Messaging Runtime Sync" for handling synchronous Content-Based-Routing scenarios
  • New template "Pipeline Template Step01 - Inbound Processing Synchronous" for scenario-specific synchronous inbound processing
  • New template "Pipeline Template Step07 - Outbound Processing Synchronous" for scenario-specific synchronous outbound processing
  • Generic inbound processing for XI supports both asynchronous and synchronous messaging
  • Failed test messages end with escalation instead of completed
  • Added custom error handling for generic XI and IDoc inbound flows

Version 1.0.10: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/new-pipeline-concept-features-supporting-service-interface-operations-and/ba-p/14049909

  • Supporting service interface operations
  • Updated the allowed header list
  • Have done script optimizations
  • Converted the XI message ID to upper case

Version 1.0.11: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/new-pipeline-for-cloud-integration-features-with-version-1-0-11/ba-p/14093335

  • The search based on IDoc number in the message monitor has been improved by removing the leading zeros from IDoc number in ApplicationID and custom header properties.
  • For the XI inbound flow, the partner ID is determined also based on service interface namespace in addition to system name and service interface name.
  • We fixed an error with wrong inbound queue in case of custom error handling for the integrated messaging runtime.
  • We introduced a configuration option to switch off the option to determine the partner ID using the so called "tilde option". The default is that this option is switched off.
  • We changed the default inbound processing queue of the XI inbound and the IDoc inbound flows from PIPQ01 to PIPX01 so that by default the integrated messaging runtime is used.

Version 1.0.12: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/new-pipeline-concept-features-with-version-1-0-12-improved-handling-of/ba-p/14112869

  • Improved handling of custom header properties
  • New processing option: Using exchange properties
  • Previous Approach Still Supported: Manually Setting customHeaderProperties

Support by migration tool: https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/migration-tooling-now-supporting-migration-via-the-pipeline-approach/ba-p/14073721

entry.title: Re: How SAP CX AI Toolkit Integrations Create Real Business Value
entry.content:
Thanks for this insightful breakdown of the SAP CX AI Toolkit and its ecosystem of integrations — the way you tie each connection to real, practical business outcomes makes it much easier to see where ROI and productivity gains come from. Summarizing email threads for sales meetings, unifying customer profiles via CDP, and surfacing proactive service recommendations are exactly the kinds of outcomes that help teams work smarter instead of just harder. This kind of integration-led value is what turns AI from a buzzword into a daily business assistant.

How do you measure the impact of these AI-driven integrations in practice — specifically, what KPIs or success metrics do SAP customers typically track to validate business value post-deployment?

 

For example:

• Do organizations benchmark increases in sales productivity or conversion rates?

• Is there a standard way to quantify time saved in support case handling or meeting prep?

• Are there common scorecards for customer experience improvement tied to these AI integrations?

 

Understanding how value is measured and reported would help customers better plan, assess, and justify adoption.

entry.title: Re: Impact of Not Settling a Maintenance Order in SAP S/4...
entry.content:
Dear @JK_,

TECO is not a financial close and the order can still get costs. SAP explicitly states that after Technical Completion the system reduces open PRs/reservations/capacities but the order can continue to receive costs for example given invoice receipts for delivered/used materials it’s blocked for changes, not blocked for all follow-on cost postings

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-asset-management/explaining-operational-overhead-maintenance-4wm-

So TECO without settlement is normal but it leaves the order as an open cost collector

If you don’t settle the order keeps an open balance. SAP describes settlement as the step that transfers costs from the maintenance order to receivers and the balance on the order becomes zero

If you skip settlement:

  • Debit balance remains on the maintenance order
  • Costs are not transferred to the intended receiver commonly the reference object cost center but also WBS or fixed asset

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-asset-management/explaining-period-end-closing-maintenance-orders-bf7-_ffb678d5-4ed4-49fe-aac1-2fd5ac6fc4c5

SAP is very clear that the settlement profile of the order type governs whether full settlement is required before business completion/closing

Option A: If your maintenance order types are processed according to phases

Default behavior:

  • Actual costs must be settled in full
  • The order must be fully settled for all relevant ledgers before business completion and closing
  • Otherwise you get error message when you try to close the order

Option B: If the order type is not processed according to phases

SAP business completion can be performed even if not fully settled but:

  • costs may remain on the order and
  • you cannot settle further costs after it has been completed for business

This is a key risk if you business complete/close too early and then late invoices arrive, you can end up with costs you can’t settle unless you reverse the status / correct the situation

Financial reporting vs controlling reporting:

  • The expense postings still exist in FI/Universal Journal settlement is mainly about moving them to the correct receiver (cost center/WBS/asset) for management accounting and downstream processes
  • Controlling / cost center reporting will be wrong or incomplete until settlement is done because the costs remain parked on the order instead of the final receiver
  • If the receiver is a fixed asset delaying settlement can delay correct capitalization-related postings

Open balances at closing:

SAP guidance frames period-end closing for maintenance orders precisely as settling to receivers for financial reporting and zeroing the order balance, so leaving orders unsettled at month/year-end typically means:

  • open balances remain on maintenance orders
  • extra reconciliation effort
  • and potentially reopening periods later if you need settlement postings and the period is already closed process/control risk

Best practice in Public Cloud:

SAP recommended operating model is:

  • maintenance orders are usually settled automatically at regular intervals via a background job or manually if not set up
  • Use the appropriate settlement tooling SAP provides Run Settlement Actual to settle actual costs from an order to receivers

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c56f622a2edf491b9f1b596b55587009/65511a912abd45518fdab7d995304e47.html

Practical safe rule:

1. TECO when work is done expect late invoices

2. Keep settling periodically and do a final settlement at month-end before you attempt business completion

3. Only CLSD once you’re confident no more relevant costs will arrive or your setup still allows settlement after business completion but SAP warns you may not be able to

Best Regards,

Dawid
